commonIssues: Root and crown rot in moist, poorly drained soils or over a wet winter; the single biggest killer. Use free-draining soil and never let the pot stay waterlogged.
French tarragon is tender in cold, wet winters and may not overwinter outdoors in damp climates; grow in a container and shelter it over winter, or apply a straw/leaf mulch in cold zones.
Loses vigour and flavour after 3-4 years; divide the clump every 3-4 years to keep it productive.

In a container the roots quickly become root-bound and flavour declines; repot or divide every 2-3 years. Container minimum (~6 L, a 20 cm / 8 in wide and deep pot per RHS) suits one plant for a season; a larger 8-12 L pot is better for a multi-year plant.
True French tarragon is sterile and cannot be grown from seed; it is only propagated from cuttings or root division. Seed sold as 'tarragon' is usually the coarser, less flavourful Russian tarragon.
In regions where summer highs exceed about 32 C (90 F), give afternoon/partial shade to improve survival.
Foliage is toxic to dogs, cats, and horses if eaten.

source.citation: Utah State University Extension - French Tarragon in the Garden: full sun (partial shade where summer temps exceed 90 F / 32 C); water established plants once a week and let soil dry between waterings; well-drained soil, drought tolerant; low fertilizer needs, fertilize only at planting; space 24 in apart; winter hardy to zone 4; plant after last spring frost; generally insect and disease free but prone to root rot from overwatering.

Utah State University Extension - French Tarragon in the Garden: 'French tarragon has low fertilizer requirements' and 'only fertilize when planting'; basis for an infrequent (~60-day) light feeding interval.

Utah State University Extension - French Tarragon in the Garden: winter hardy to zone 4 (cold-hardy minimum approx -29 C); partial shading recommended where summer temperatures exceed 90 F (32 C), basis for heat-stress threshold; plant after last spring frost.

NC State Extension Gardener Plant Toolbox - Artemisia dracunculus: 'Full sun (6 or more hours of direct sunlight a day)'; prefers good drainage / dry conditions; drought tolerant; USDA zones 3a-8b; height 1-3 ft, width 1-1.5 ft, clumping erect herbaceous perennial; susceptible to root or crown rot in moist, poorly drained soils; toxic to dogs, cats and horses.

NC State Extension Gardener Plant Toolbox - Artemisia dracunculus: USDA hardiness zones 3a-8b; clumping, erect, herbaceous perennial habit (basis for growthHabit 'upright' and no support); foliage toxic to pets.

University of Illinois Extension - Tarragon (French): full sun, tolerates some light shade; soil must be well-drained, will not tolerate poorly drained soils especially over winter; divide every 3-4 years; small pots can be grown where bright light is available, kept on the dry side; true French tarragon is sterile and grown only from cuttings or division.

Royal Horticultural Society (RHS) - How to grow Tarragon: grow in full sun in free-draining soil or a container; for a container use one 'at least 20cm (8in) wide and deep for one plant' with plenty of drainage holes (basis for ~6 L recommended minimum); French tarragon 50-75 cm tall, 30 cm wide; plant mid-spring to early summer; harvest shoot tips spring to autumn; French tarragon tender in wet UK winters, best containerised and sheltered over winter; avoid overwatering as it dislikes damp.

Royal Horticultural Society (RHS) - How to grow Tarragon: harvest from spring to autumn taking young shoot tips; plant mid-spring to early summer; winter dormancy (basis for productiveMonths / plantingWindows / dormancyMonths).

Confidence note: feedingProfile.baseIntervalDays (60) is estimated. Extension sources (USU) state tarragon is a low feeder needing fertiliser essentially only at planting, and RHS gives no feeding schedule, so no source specifies an exact recurring interval; 60 days is a conservative low-rate interval consistent with 'low fertilizer requirements'. hotWeatherMultiplier (1.5) is also an estimate - sources confirm increased water need in hot/dry spells and the once-weekly baseline but give no explicit multiplier.
